concept.headline: In Kotlin, if and when are expressions — they return values. when replaces switch and if-else chains with readable, exhaustive branching.
concept.explanation: Kotlin's if is not just a statement — it returns the value of the chosen branch, so you can use it on the right side of a val. when is a powerful multi-branch expression: branches can match exact values, ranges (in 1..10), type checks (is String), or arbitrary boolean conditions (when used without an argument). Kotlin's compiler enforces exhaustiveness when when is used as an expression — you must cover all cases or add else.
keyPoints: if (cond) a else b — returns a or b; can replace ternary operator
when(x) { val -> expr; else -> expr } — matches x against branches
when branches: exact value, comma list, in range, is Type, else
when without argument works like a readable if-else chain

demo.intro: Watch a grade function use when as an expression to return a letter grade from a score.

explanation.what: grade(85) called — score = 85 inside the function
explanation.why: We test a score in the 80s range
explanation.intent: Show when matching in order from top to bottom
explanation.misconception: when without an argument evaluates each branch's boolean condition top-to-bottom, stopping at the first true one — just like if-else if chains but more readable.

explanation.intent: Show sequential evaluation of when branches
explanation.misconception: when does not evaluate ALL branches — it stops at the first matching one. Branches below a match are never checked.

explanation.intent: Show the matching branch returning its value
explanation.misconception: There is no break statement needed — unlike C switch, Kotlin's when does not fall through to subsequent branches

explanation.why: grade returned 'B' — the when expression value
explanation.intent: Confirm when expression returned a value to the caller
explanation.misconception: grade's return type is String. when is the entire body (expression body) — its result is the function's return value.

explanation.why: Testing a score below all thresholds
explanation.misconception: Without an else, when as an expression would be a compile error when score doesn't match any branch — Kotlin requires exhaustiveness

explanation.why: 62 did not satisfy any of the three conditions above
explanation.intent: Show else as the catch-all branch
explanation.misconception: else in when is like a default case in switch — it handles everything not matched above

explanation.why: grade returned 'F' from the else branch
explanation.misconception: F here means no threshold was met — the else is exhaustive and always runs when no other branch matches

prediction.question:
What is the output?

val x = 3
val label = if (x % 2 == 0) "even" else "odd"
println(label)
options: Compile error — if cannot return a value
prediction.explanation: 3 % 2 = 1, which is not 0, so the else branch returns 'odd'. In Kotlin, if is an expression — it returns the value of the matched branch, just like a ternary operator in other languages.

explanation.afterDemo: Because if and when are expressions, you write less code and avoid intermediate mutable variables. when is especially powerful — it reads like documentation: 'when score is at least 90, grade is A'. The compiler also helps: when used as an expression must be exhaustive, catching missing cases at compile time.
keyTakeaways: if is an expression in Kotlin: val x = if (a > b) a else b replaces the ternary operator.
when branches top-to-bottom and stops at the first match — no fall-through, no break.
when as an expression must be exhaustive — add else to cover all remaining cases.

practice.prompt: Write a function describe(n: Int): String that uses when to return: "negative" if n < 0, "zero" if n == 0, "small" if n in 1..9, "large" otherwise. Print describe(-5), describe(0), describe(7), describe(42).
practice.hint: Use when { } without an argument. Each branch is a boolean condition. Use in 1..9 for the range check.

feedback.successMessage: Correct! Each when branch evaluates its condition and returns the matching string.
retryHints: Use when { n < 0 -> ... } — no argument after when, conditions are boolean
Expected output (one per line): negative, zero, small, large

summary.title: Control Flow — Complete
takeaways: if is an expression: val x = if (a) b else c — no ternary operator needed.
when replaces switch and if-else chains. Branches can be values, ranges, or conditions.
when as an expression is exhaustive — the compiler requires an else if cases are not complete.
